author: @terryharris3393
text: This is a J-tube burner not a rocket stove. The J-tube burner is a part of a rocket stove and may be necessary but by itself not sufficient. Most of the energy in burning is exhausted up your riser and as such does not allow the stove to approach the 1800-2100 F temperatures that a proper rocket stove achieves. Also, you are placing the mass to be heated in the wrong place. Please read Ianto Evans’ book to understand his invention and why it’s called a rocket stove. When you get the rocket part you’ll understand why a J-tube burner is not a rocket stove.

author: @michaelmoorrees3585
text: I bet that puppy's got a good draft ! When camping, we often make "stump stoves", where we drill two right angle holes (in a wooden log), that make the same "L"  shaped draft channel as this rocket stove. Only difference, is the stump stove is its own fuel, hence one-time use; disposable. But the stump stove works surprisingly well, as I suspect this rocket stove does, also.

author: @woodworks2123
text: Robert I've been obsessed with these for years, I have built several just with tins plus a perlite jacket but I was interested in heating water with it. Copying videos I saw, I got some microbore copper pipe and made a coil, put it in the stove, then connected it to a house radiator then   it went into an open topped water cylinder (open so pressure didn't build up. This created a thermisyphon and the rad now heats my greenhouse in the winter. I'm looking for a better stove part though and make it self feeding  with an auger. Slot of work still but I have the basic concept working well. Perhaps 1 solution for these ridiculous costs to heat our homes. I'd maybe add a small pump instead of relying on the thermisyphon, it can sometimes get really hot. A pumps flow could be adjusted to control this better.

author: @MrChris20912
text:
Cool way to put the welding skills to work. And will look forward to seeing what you make with the Mass Heater version. 

Seen the more commonly described inverted oil drum configuration and the oil drum plus stove pipe running through fire bricks or cob. 

There's another out there which might fit a bit better with both the workshop space and your joy of welding, which is to zig-zag the flue pipe section and attach a metal plate to one side to maximize radiant heat energy (There's a company with sells these as space heaters, and a pellet bin so it can be filled once roughly every 8 hours). 

Also, seen a version of this made with a straight vertical flue and horizontally attached steel tubes, but only as a one off, not as a commercial product.

The main reason to go with one of these alternatives (besides simplicity) over the oil drum version is that the main failure point for the oil drum is the thin steel they are made from tends to corrode and break down over a season to two - though this is usually mainly due to the variations in combustion material and dirty burning adding fun chemistries to the mix of gasses moving through the drum area.

For a mass heater, you may also want to consider a burn box with a secondary air feed tube, rather than an open direct feed like first iteration. With a burn box, the air flow is easier to control, simpler to manage, and an added measure of fire safety.

author: @aspudkicker
text: Like you say there are many ways to create a rocket stove. Ive built a number of different designs. The best one I built is the same shape as yours but had a number of differences. The 1st main difference was the bottom piece had a flap on to stop air going in as I wanted the fuel chamber to receive full draw from the burn chamber, which means all the combustion gases and combustion air is drawn through the middle section The botom section was only used for ash collection. The 2nd main difference is I started to make mine with a double skin with the gap between filled with perlite, I did this because the combustion chamber is hotter, burning more of the particulates, the other bonus of this is if you keep the gases between 400-600 degrees C you can also burn the carbon monoxide releasing more heat energy. I decided not to put it into a gas cylinder/heat exchanger and install into my garage in the end, because there were to many flamable things in my garage including the felt roof. Im currently altering my work van load area to fit a £90 chinese diesel cab heater. If this works out ok I will do the same for my garage, Ive seen others on YT do this and red diesel is allowed to be used as fuel for heat.

author: @norfolknchance.500
text:
Brilliant! ... I've made versions in the past using "J" pipe enclosed in an old metal electrical box to contain cat litter ( Fuller's earth) as insulation around the whole thing, including first part of vertical pipe, insulation around burn chamber area of stove increases efficiency and output to the point that my stainless flu pipe was glowing cherry red, over about 6ft, and an 18in blue jet of flame exiting the chimney outside! .... Was only used to heat garage, but incredible!
Had to learn to gauge very small amount of fuel required to keep "bunson burner" effect from chimney outside, so as not to worry neighbours etc! Haha!

Now moved on to waste oil burners, ...... again simple construction from scrap etc, (plenty vids on here!) and oil has very high calorific value, and when burned with the same "excess or forced air" principal does not even produce smoke from chimney, so simple and cheap to build a burner, and waste oil is often free from garages and chip shops etc!
Also possible to incorporate pipework to provide endless free hot water!
Worth a look!
Cheers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I will be looking mate - cheers - I was also thinking about a hot water system incorporated in this

replies.author: @norfolknchance.500
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Cool, hope it gives you another angle on things that you may not of been aware of!
On another related note, free fuel, that is, are you aware of the very simple, safe and cheap process of converting all this waste plastic we produce, into diesel and even petrol, with some tweaking of the process, by way of pyrolysis!?
A crazy simple and cheap way to produce free fuel, no skills required really!
Also its cleaner fuel than we buy from the forecourt, as contains no sulphur!

Isn't it funny that our governments are not aware of this simple process, which also solves the plastic problem, perhaps they should watch some of the videos on YouTube!? Haha!

The subject of burners and fuel, and its uses/production is right up my street, and could go on, as you can probably guess, but I'll leave it with you, be great to hear your opinion and perhaps share some knowledge once you have chance to explore and experiment etc yourself!?

Thanks again!

author: @kevinleebailey
text: A real simple stove can be made using a ring pull baked beans can with a peas or carrots can and a evaporated milk can opener. Just punch holes around the base of each can and smaller holes around the top of the smaller peas can. Then slide the pea can into the baked beans can and you are done. To make a more professional finish use a drill to make the holes in the cans. Another improvement would be to drill small holes in the bottom of each can in a grid pattern so ash can fall through the bottom in to another baked beans can to catch the ash.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: These can be adapted to many variations, I have seen them with thick mesh like steel welded at the top to radiate the heat and be used for cooking with a hood about 40-50cm above to channel any potential smoke into a pipe chimney leading outdoors. Also using strips of thick steel welded to the burner gives additional mass for extra heat to radiate and heat the workshop.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice mate - thanks for the ideas - cheers

replies.author: @norfolknchance.500
replies.text:
With due respect, the "mesh and strips" idea sounds strange and I would think would be very inefficient!
A much simpler and effective way is to incorporate a cylinder ( repurposed gas bottle) just above the stove itself, forming part of the chimey/flu, which would act as a simple heat sink and emit heat into the surroundings!
Alternatively, pipe the chimney horizontally before it's exit, which in itself creates a heat 
They can also be built into floor in garage/shed/earthbag structures etc, which allows horizontal piping of chimney/flu, and so creates simple under floor heating!

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @norfolknchance.500 I have seen them with vertical pipes around the burner and with vertical thick strips of steel. Both transfer the heat from the burner much like a heat sink on a transistor.

replies.author: @norfolknchance.500
replies.text:
@stevetobias4890 Fair do's ..... as I previously stated, it does sound less effective than a thin walled vessel, with a large surface area in terms of a heat sink!
Basic physics tells me the principle you mention would work to a degree, but can certainly think of better ways to get more bang for my buck!
Those you have seen carrying out that principle have probably learnt a lot more since they used that idea!
Nothing wrong with learning though, and as the "Fun Boy Three" once said, it ain't what you do, it's the way that you do it! 👊✌

author: @cynthiamathieu5862
text:
Up to 60% of the BTU's are in the volatiles (creosote, smoke, etc.) however it takes over 1300F to completely burn them. Steel burns at 1000F. This is where the increased efficiency comes from, burning all the gasses. 

So any rocket heater made from steel will either burn up after short usage or is not reaching the efficiency rocket heaters are capable of. If you put a gas analyzer on your heater you would find it is a far way off from the 85% - 90% efficiency rocket heaters are known for.  My guess is you would be doing good, to be over 50%. Building with steel is very popular, but they are rocket heaters in name only. Standard stoves are usually only 20-25% efficient. BTW

The combustion chambers need to be made from refractory/or clay and be well insulated with proper chamber designs to maintain burn temperatures 1800F - 2200F. This will give you a heater with good 2ndary air ratios and turbulence for gas mixing that will maintain 88-90% efficiency through the entire burn.


We have been building and testing rocket heaters for nearly 10 years.

replies.author: @taylorwestmore4664
replies.text: What's your take on the "vortex rocket engine" design? Can it be adapted for solid fuel in a rocket stove? The air inlet on the combustion chamber is at the base of the output nozzle, so preheated air swirls upwards around the fuel to keep the chamber cooler than the combustion temperature.

replies.author: @al3xf103
replies.text: Hi there, I appreciate you may well be experts in this area, but I'd love to see some sources documenting the numbers you have quoted. I had already researched the subject briefly but hadn't understood that the temperatures needed to be that high, particular as many designs use oil drums to dissipate the heat. Many thanks in advance..

replies.author: @fireballxl-5748
replies.text: WRONG..... the melting point of steel is 2500 deg F or 1400+ deg C.   You are way off.  10 years of building and testing and you still can't get the math right?    LOL

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have to agree refractory is probably the best option for a permanent install and I have seen some lovely designs using firebrick and I was contemplating it - I went for steel because of the adaptability of it, cheapness, portability and - for me - ease of use. But, I have to point out 1,300F is only 704C - propane/butane mixes - which are the one of the hottest burning gas mixes - burns at around 800C. 1,000F is around 537C - aluminium melts at around 600C - maybe you were thinking of that? The melting point of steel is around 1,500C which is about  2,800F - constant heating and cooling of mild steel at around 1,000C or 1,850F will cause spalling but I would think that that's unlikely to be a problem with 4mm plate for a good few years

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - but I must mention this and please do take it in the way it was meant and not as a criticism of you but rather as a pointer to help encourage others to post and to take part in discussions across the channel - I really think we need to be respectful of others and I think just saying folks are wrong isn't the way to approach this - al3xf gave a good reply and I don't think mine was too bad either. Cheers and thank you

author: @R3dp055um

Another good one, Robert.
Not to be a smartass, but if I was building it for heat, I'd make it with fire bricks in the first place, and line it with clay in place of mortar. That way I'd have the mass all in one piece. 
Plus, I'm not a welder :)

author: @yoda-be.at1746
text:
The rocket stoves I have seen use a ceramic for the combustion chamber which is insulated and the heat is taken from the exhaust. The idea is to try not to dissipate any heat at the combustion chamber so to achieve maximum combustion temperature. The stack is also insulated to about 1/2 meter up. The draw through it is so strong that the stack can be covered by an oil drum and the exhaust exits at the bottom/side. This design is used in the mass rocket stove heater where the exhaust runs horizontally and covered by a mass to heat up.

Lol, yep commented before the end (face palm) 🤓

replies.author: @nickkitchener6155
replies.text: That's a super important factor for high efficiency combustion and a good solid draft with low chimney heights.
